name: Device & Hardware Sensors
description: Network Information, Battery Status, and the Vibration API: three Web APIs that read the device's real state — connection, battery, and haptic feedback — to adapt the experience in real time.
longDescription: Three Web APIs that give the app awareness of the user's hardware and network: the Network Information API exposes the connection type, the estimated quality (effectiveType), and data-saver mode (saveData); the Battery Status API exposes charge level and charging state, though it now has limited browser support due to fingerprinting and privacy concerns; and the Vibration API triggers haptic feedback through vibration patterns. Together, they show how to adapt behavior — not just appearance — to the device's real conditions: dropping video quality on slow connections, saving energy on low battery, or confirming an action with vibration.
tags: Network Information API, Battery Status API, Vibration API
architecture: Reactive reads of hardware Browser APIs, with change-listeners
technologies: Network Information API, Battery Status API, Vibration API
concepts: Adaptive UX based on network/energy conditions, haptic feedback
networkDesc1: The Network Information API is a browser API that exposes information about the user's internet connection.
networkDesc2: In practice, it helps web apps and PWAs understand the quality and type of connection available on the device.
networkCapabilitiesItem1: whether the user is online or offline;
networkCapabilitiesItem2: the type of connection in use;
networkAdaptNote: This lets an app adapt its behavior based on the quality of the internet connection.
networkExamplesItem1: lower the quality of videos and images;
networkExamplesItem4: improve the experience on slow connections.
networkPwaNote: In PWAs, this API helps build smarter, more resilient apps that adapt to different network conditions.
networkUnsupportedMessage: The Network Information API isn't supported in this browser (only Chromium implements it; Firefox and Safari don't).
batteryDesc1: The Battery Status API is a browser API that exposes information about the device's battery.
batteryDesc2: In practice, it lets web apps detect battery level, charging state, and changes in energy consumption.
batteryCapabilitiesItem2: detect whether the device is charging;
batteryExamplesItem1: lower the frequency of background syncs;
batterySupportNote: Despite being useful, this API has limited support today — several modern browsers have restricted or removed it over privacy and fingerprinting concerns.
batteryValueNote: Even so, it shows how web apps can access information increasingly close to what a native app can get.
batteryDischargingTimeLabel: Estimated time until the battery is fully drained:
batteryUnsupportedMessage: The Battery Status API isn't supported in this browser (removed from Firefox and never implemented in Safari, over privacy and fingerprinting concerns).
vibrationDesc1: The Vibration API is a browser API that lets web apps trigger the device's vibration motor.
vibrationDesc2: In practice, it lets you create tactile responses to user actions, making the experience more interactive.
vibrationCapabilitiesItem2: provide feedback on buttons and interactions;
vibrationCapabilitiesItem4: improve games and mobile app experiences.
vibrationPatternNote: Vibration can follow simple patterns or more complex sequences, with multiple pulses separated by pauses.
vibrationSupportNote: This API is mostly supported on mobile devices, since most desktops and laptops don't have vibration hardware.
vibrationPwaNote: In PWAs, the Vibration API helps bring the web experience closer to the feel of using a native app.
vibrationHowToUseIntro: Click "Start vibration" and feel the device vibrate. To stop, click "Stop vibration".
vibrationUnsupportedMessage: The Vibration API isn't supported on this device or browser.
vibrationAcceptedMessage: ✓ navigator.vibrate() returned true — the call was accepted by the browser.
vibrationRejectedMessage: ✗ navigator.vibrate() returned false — the call was rejected. Check Chrome's permissions on MIUI (Settings → Apps → Chrome → Other permissions → Vibrate).
vibrationMethod1: navigator.vibrate(duration) — triggers the device's vibration for a specific duration, in milliseconds
vibrationMethod2: navigator.vibrate([on, off, on]) — runs a vibration pattern alternating vibration and pause periods
